data: A dataset of 19,611 crowdfunding campaigns (projects) collected from Indiegogo.com between 2010 and 2021. The data included campaign ID, title, blurb, category, money raised, target amount, updates, videos, creator experience, social links, progress, and cover images. An additional sample of 8,511 campaigns from Kickstarter.com was used for robustness testing.
method: Image content orientation was initially identified using classical grounded theory coding (open, axial, selective coding, theoretical saturation test) and then classified using a deep learning model (ResNet-34 residual neural network). Narrative emotion was explored using text mining techniques, combining linguistic inquiry and word count (LIWC) and a naïve Bayes model to categorize narratives as emotional or not emotional. The impact on funding performance was estimated using a logit model, with Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) for robustness checks using alternative dependent variables. Propensity Score Matching (PSM) was employed to address endogeneity concerns.
results: Product-oriented images have a more positive effect on funding performance than other cover image orientations. Word-oriented images negatively impact funding performance. Narratives’ emotions generally promote campaign success. When narratives are emotional, word-oriented images have a greater positive impact on funding performance than people-oriented images, which in turn outperform product-oriented images. Cover image content orientation has a stronger impact on funding performance for technological and innovative campaigns, specifically enhancing the positive impact of product-oriented images.

tech_capa_operationalization: Stock of granted patents (Stockpatent) and founders’ years of university-level technical and scientific education (Techeduc).
funding_success_operationalization: Logarithm of firm sales (LnSize) and logarithm of the number of employees (LnSize), capturing the growth rate of new technology-based firms (NTBFs).
signal_vehicles: patents, founder's technical education, sales growth
mediation_moderation_effects: The paper explicitly examines that the type of venture capital investor (IVC vs. CVC) moderates the effect of VC investment on the growth mode of portfolio firms (sales vs. employment). It is found that grandstanding incentives mediate the relationship between IVC investor type and short-term sales growth of portfolio firms.
industry_sector: high-tech (manufacturing and services), computers, electronic components, telecommunications equipment, optical instruments, medical instruments, electronic instruments, biotechnology, pharmaceuticals, advanced materials, robotics, process automation equipment, software, Internet, telecommunications services
data: A unique longitudinal dataset of 531 Italian new technology-based firms (NTBFs) observed over a 10-year period (1994–2003), yielding 3,686 firm-year observations on employees and 3,628 on sales. The sample was drawn from the Research on Entrepreneurship in Advanced Technologies (RITA), 2004 directory, and includes 47 IVC- or CVC-backed firms. Data on VC investments, firm characteristics, and founder human capital were collected via a survey and public/commercial databases.
method: The study employs an augmented Gibrat-law dynamic panel data model. Generalized Method of Moments (GMM) estimation techniques, specifically GMM-system (GMM-SYS) estimates, were used to control for the endogeneity of VC investments. Additional methods included a semi-parametric Cox competing-risks model for the selection equation and a Heckman two-step sample selection approach. Robustness checks included a Semykina and Wooldridge (2010) test for survivorship bias.
results: Both IVC and CVC investments have a positive and sizeable long-term treatment effect on the sales and employment growth of portfolio firms. IVC investments have a significantly greater positive short-term treatment effect on sales growth (182% greater for IVC-backed firms one year after investment) compared to CVC investments, which show a more gradual contribution. This difference in sales growth effect, though positive, tends to vanish after the fourth year post-investment. The treatment effect of IVC investments on short-term employment growth is smaller than on sales growth. There is no statistically significant difference found between IVC-backed and CVC-backed firms in the post-investment employment growth path. Furthermore, IVC-backed firms show a significant reduction in the ratio of value added to sales, consistent with increased outsourcing to sustain sales growth, while CVC-backed firms do not exhibit this effect. These findings support the grandstanding hypothesis, where IVC investors accelerate sales growth to signal their investment ability.

title: Venture capital investor type and the growth mode of new technology-based firms

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes 'technological capabilities' as a firm's potential for invention and innovation, which can lead to commercial gains and contribute to firm performance and market value. This 'unobservable quality' or 'potential' is difficult for external investors to assess in knowledge-intensive industries.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized through 'patent activity,' specifically measured by the 'number of granted patents a firm has received' and the 'number of patent applications it has filed'. These are considered signals of a firm's quality and potential.
funding_success_definition: The paper implicitly defines 'funding success' as the ability of emerging firms to attract external investment, specifically venture capital, and to increase the amounts of capital received in sequential financing rounds.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized as 'the natural log of the total amount of VC funding raised by a given biotechnology firm in round 1 (model 1) or round 2 (model 2)'.
signal_vehicles: patent applications, granted patents, founder signal
mediation_moderation_effects: The interaction term 'patents granted before round 1 × universities in the MSA' was explicitly examined but found not statistically significant. Similarly, 'patents granted between rounds × universities in the MSA' was also not significant.
financing_stage: first_round, second_round, seed, early_stage, expansion_stage, later_stage
data: A longitudinal dataset of more than 580 U.S.-based dedicated biotechnology firms (DBFs) from 2001 to 2011 was used. Data sources include Thomson Reuter’s SDC Platinum Database (for venture capital investments, firm addresses, funding rounds, and investor information) and Google Patents® (for granted patents and patent applications). Biographical information for academic founders was gathered from firm and personal websites. Regional data, such as the number of universities in an MSA, was sourced from the National Institutes of Health and related organizations. The unit of analysis is individual biotechnology firm financing rounds.
method: The study employs two empirical models (one for each financing round) using regression analysis. White’s heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors and standard errors clustered at the state level were used to address heteroskedasticity and potential correlation of errors. Robustness checks included Heckman Selection Models to account for potential selection bias, and a pooled regression model with firm and year fixed effects. Alternative measures for patent economic value (patent family size) were also used for robustness.
results: Patent applications before the first round of financing significantly increase the amount of funds raised (by 7.7% per additional application, estimated at $557,333 at the average first-round funding). Patent activity (both applications and granted patents) before the second round of financing does not significantly affect funding, indicating that the signaling value of patents diminishes as information asymmetries between investors and firms decrease. The study found that patent applications had a more significant signaling role than granted patents individually. The founder signal (distinguished academic or prior founding experience) significantly increased funding only in the first round. Investments by larger, wealthy syndicated VCFs and those in closer proximity to the target firm were associated with higher capital investments. Proximity effects from VCFs and local patents positively influenced only the first round of financing. Interaction effects between patents and local universities were not statistically significant.

tech_capa_definition: The paper does not explicitly define "technological capabilities." funding_success_definition: A start-up achieves "funding success" if it successfully exits through an Initial Public Offering (IPO) or an acquisition by an existing firm.
funding_success_operationalization: For the type of successful exit, it is measured by the binary dependent variable `IPO` (1 if IPO exit, 0 if acquisition exit). For the overall rate of successful exits, it is measured by the inverse of the `Failure` variable, where `Failure` is a binary variable (1 if no IPO or acquisition exit, 0 otherwise).

mediation_moderation_effects: The type of VC fund (CVC vs. IVC) indirectly impacts the exit strategy, with investment amount positively mediating the likelihood of an IPO exit, and duration negatively mediating it. The effect of duration on the probability of an IPO exit is non-linear, with a positive quadratic term suggesting the negative effect is stronger for shorter durations.
industry_sector: high-tech, business service, chemicals and allied products, industrial machinery and equipment, electronic equipment, communications, engineering services
data: Cross-sectional data on 4,311 successful U.S. start-ups with first investment year from 1980 to 2004 for Hypotheses 1-3. An enlarged dataset of 7,432 successful and unsuccessful U.S. start-ups (1980-2004) is used for Hypothesis 4. Data sources include the ThomsonOne private equity database, Securities Data Company (SDC) Global New Issues Database, NBER Patent Citation database, Compustat for financial data, and Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) for stock market information. The unit of analysis is the start-up level.
method: The study develops a theoretical model of investment, duration, and exit strategies. Empirical analysis employs Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression, logistic regression, parametric survival models (based on Weibull distribution), treatment-effects models (with a Probit first stage), and instrumental variable (IV) estimation.
results: CVC-backed start-ups receive larger investment amounts and have longer durations before exiting compared to IVC-backed start-ups. A higher investment amount significantly increases the likelihood of an IPO exit, while a longer duration significantly decreases it. The type of VC fund (CVC vs. IVC) does not directly influence the exit decision when investment and duration effects are controlled for, indicating that investment and duration mediate the VC type's influence. Start-ups with more patent applications (higher quality/innovativeness) before VC investment have a higher probability of an IPO exit. Although the theoretical model predicted a positive effect of investment amount on the overall rate of successful exits, this empirical finding was not statistically significant, possibly due to data quality limitations.

data: Theoretical model with risk-neutral entrepreneurs and investors in a competitive market. Ventures are characterized by an unobservable success probability (p) uniformly distributed between 0 and 1. The model also incorporates a continuous distribution over access costs (c) for entrepreneurs to access VCs.
method: Theoretical signaling model using pure-strategy Perfect-Bayesian Equilibria with binary threshold rules.
results: When ventures are ex-ante identical, entrepreneurs retain larger expected ownership shares and prefer angel financing over VC financing. When entrepreneurs are ex-ante heterogeneous with private information, a separating equilibrium exists where entrepreneurs with higher likelihoods of success choose VC financing in the first stage, while those with lower likelihoods of success choose angel financing. tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes technological capabilities as the scientific or technical merit of a startup's technology, its potential to advance to commercialization, and the scientific capability of its staff. Grant-winning ventures are identified as 'technologically competent' with the ability to 'make the transition to market-oriented norms'.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized by 'PATENTS', a logarithmically transformed continuous variable that counts the cumulative number of patent applications a startup has made since its founding, serving as an 'objective measure of firms' technical outputs'.
funding_success_definition: The paper implicitly defines funding success as a startup's 'ability to attract subsequent venture capital (VC) funding'.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized by binary indicator variables (VC_1-2Q, VC_3-4Q, and VC_5-6Q) set to 1 if a startup receives any VC funding within specific two-quarter periods (first two quarters, quarters three and four, or quarters five and six) following a focal quarter, and 0 otherwise.
signal_vehicles: government research grants, patents
mediation_moderation_effects: The number of patents (PATENTS) moderates the relationship between receiving a government grant (GRANT) and subsequent VC funding. Specifically, the signaling benefit of a government grant is stronger and more pronounced for startups with fewer or no patents, indicating a substitution effect where the grant compensates for a lack of patenting activity.
industry_sector: clean energy, biofuels, energy efficiency, geothermal, materials, solar, storage, tidal, wind, fuel cells, membranes, smart grid
data: The study uses a sample of U.S. based clean energy startups from 2005 to 2011. Data was extracted from Energy Acuity's Power Database, cross-checked with Cleantech Group's i3 Platform, Bloomberg's New Energy Finance's Insight Data, company websites, and Bloomberg Businessweek Private Firm database. Information on government grants was confirmed with www.grants.gov, and patent data from the PatentEdge database. The sample includes startups less than ten years old as of 2010, with commercial aspirations and intent to seek VC funding. The unit of analysis is quarterly panel data for each qualifying startup.
method: The research employs a propensity score analysis approach to address endogeneity, constructing a matched sample using logistic regression and the nearest neighbor matching technique. The effects of receiving a grant on follow-on VC funding are then estimated using a second set of logistic regressions. Robustness tests included multinomial logit models, discrete time event history models, and continuous time Cox hazard models.
results: Startups that received government research grants were 12% more likely to obtain subsequent VC funding within the immediate two quarters compared to matched non-recipients. This signaling effect of government grants was only significant in the first two-quarter period following the grant receipt and was only significant for a startup's first grant. The signaling benefit of government grants was more pronounced for startups with fewer or no patents. A grant recipient startup with no patent applications was 7% more likely to receive follow-on VC funding than a similar startup without a grant, with this benefit decreasing as the number of patents increased (significant for startups with 0 to 2.1 logged patents, corresponding to up to seven actual patents). The findings suggest that government grants can compensate for a lack of other signals, like patents, helping less well-endowed actors attract funding.

tech_capa_definition: Green ventures are conceptualized as high-tech ventures using green technologies in their businesses or offering green products/services. Green technologies are defined as innovations including renewable energy resources, energy storage, distribution and management technologies, recycling and waste technologies, industrial processes, and technologies for capture, storage, and treatment or disposal of greenhouse gases. More broadly, they are technologies that provide, directly or indirectly, environmental benefits when compared to traditionally used technologies they replace.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized by classifying ventures as 'green' if their portfolio of (main) products/services/technologies is identified as green through manual website checks, based on definitions from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (producing goods/services that benefit the environment or conserve natural resources, or using more environment-friendly production processes or fewer natural resources).
funding_success_definition: Funding success is implicitly defined as successfully attracting venture capital investment.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized as a dummy variable, Pr(VC), which equals one if the venture has been VC-backed after its foundation.
signal_vehicles: running a business based on green technologies/products, technical/scientific education of the founder(s), functional heterogeneity of founder work experience (technical functions), patent, academic spin-out
mediation_moderation_effects: Technical/scientific education of the founder(s) (negative moderator), academic spin-out (positive moderator), and corporate shareholders (positive moderator) for the association between green business propositions and the likelihood to get VC.
industry_sector: high-tech, green technology, environmental services, renewable energy, new materials, nanotechnologies
data: A sample of 361 Italian, independent, unlisted, high-tech entrepreneurial firms less than 25 years old, founded between 1983 and 2008. Data are from the RITA (Research on Entrepreneurship in Advanced Technologies) dataset, combining survey-based questionnaires and secondary sources (Union of Italian Chambers of Commerce (Telemaco), CERVED, AIDA databases). The unit of analysis is the firm/venture.
method: The study employs probit regression models and linear instrumental variable (IV) procedures to address potential endogeneity and selection bias. An instrumental variable, 'Income for Environment' (community awareness about environmental issues from the World Values Survey), is used for identification.
results: Neither running a business based on green technologies/products nor positioning a business in a green sector per se are strongly correlated with the likelihood of receiving VC. However, a 'born-to-be-green' signal is reliable for investors only when entrepreneurs perform activities based on green technologies/products AND position their business in a green sector simultaneously (complementarity effect, +7.08% likelihood). Technical/scientific education of founders and academic spin-out origin are effective signals, while functional heterogeneity of founder work experience and patents are ineffective. Founder work experience in the same industry and serial management experience are also found to be ineffective as signals. The study also identifies moderation effects: technical/scientific education of founders has a negative moderating effect, while being an academic spin-out and having corporate shareholders positively moderates the association between green business propositions and VC likelihood.

data: A systematic literature review was conducted based on 15 selected articles from the Web of Science (WoS) database, covering the period from 1999 to September 2018. The unit of analysis is the individual scholarly article. Data extracted from these articles included their themes, objectives, main findings, and contributions regarding patents and venture capital. The reviewed articles primarily utilized secondary data from databases such as Thomson Reuter’s SDC Platinum Database, Thomson Venture Economics, PATSTAT, Crunchbase, BVCA (British Venture Capital Association), Israel Venture Capital (IVC), Dun & Bradstreet (D&B), Power Energy Acuity Database, and in some cases, primary surveys.
method: systematic_literature_review
results: The systematic review found that 11 out of 15 analyzed studies demonstrated a positive relationship between patenting and access to venture capital (VC) financing. Patents function as a reducer of information asymmetry in uncertain environments. While patent applications substantially increased funding in a startup's first round, the same positive effect was not consistently observed in subsequent financing rounds. Venture capitalists (VCs) analyze patents for initial investment decisions but may rationalize the production of new patents once an investment has been made. The effectiveness of patents as a signal is moderated by geographic distance, being more impactful when financiers and startups are geographically separated. Other quality signals identified as relevant by VCs include trademarks (which show complementarity with patents for securing higher funding), founder experience and education, strategic alliances (e.g., research contracts), government grants, and product prototypes.

title: Patents as a sign of quality for startups to achieve investments venture capital - a systematic review of the literature

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly defines technological capabilities as the underlying quality or potential of a venture's technology and its ability to develop and deploy it. It refers to 'firm quality', 'underlying quality of the firm', and 'technical qualities of the venture'. Outsiders learn about a venture's 'managerial and technological capabilities' as it ages.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized through two signal vehicles: (1) 'Number of patent applications' is used as a strong signal reflecting venture quality and competitive advantage. (2) 'Twitter sentiment' is used as a weak signal, reflecting public opinion and 'emotional states, moods, and opinions on a specific topic' (e.g., a new technology or trend) that a startup is using.
funding_success_definition: The paper conceptualizes funding success in two ways: (1) short-term 'venture valuations' at the financing date, reflecting the market value of the startup, and (2) 'long-run investment success', defined by whether the investment results in a successful exit for VCs.
funding_success_operationalization: (1) 'Pre-money valuation' (logarithm) is calculated as the product of outstanding shares and price per share prior to a financing event. (2) 'IPO dummy' is a binary variable (1 if the investment results in an IPO, 0 otherwise). (3) 'IPO+Acquisition dummy' is a binary variable (1 if the firm experiences an IPO or an acquisition, 0 otherwise).
signal_vehicles: Twitter sentiment, patent applications
mediation_moderation_effects: Moderation effects examined: venture age moderates the effects of both Twitter sentiment and patent applications on venture valuations (stronger for younger ventures). VC firm experience moderates the effect of patent applications on venture valuations (stronger for less-experienced VCs) but does not moderate the effect of Twitter sentiment. Additionally, patent applications enhance the signaling value of Twitter sentiment on venture valuations.
industry_sector: technology, 3d print, 3g, 4g, artificial intelligence, application programming interface, business intelligence, big data, bitcoin, blockchain, cleantech, cloud computing, customer relationship management, crowdfunding, digital signal processor, e-commerce, enterprise resource planning, ethernet, global positioning system, healthcare, hypertext markup language, ios, internet of things, laser, machine learning, open source, platform as a service, photovoltaic, radio-frequency identification, software as a service, semiconductor, search engine optimization, short message service, service-oriented architecture, structured query language, voice over ip, wearable, worldwide interoperability for microwave access
AI_venture: The paper includes 'artificial intelligence' as one of the 37 technologies studied. General findings for all technology-based ventures apply: Twitter sentiment (a weak signal) is positively associated with valuations but not long-term investment success, while patents (a strong signal) are associated with both. This implies that AI ventures might experience inflated valuations due to positive Twitter sentiment without a corresponding increase in long-term success.
data: Dataset of VC-backed startups in the United States. Venture data from VentureSource on 5,454 VC financing rounds by 4,005 ventures (01/01/2008-10/15/2017). Time-varying patent data from USPTO. Twitter sentiment data collected via Brandwatch, comprising 411,371 English language tweets related to 37 specified technologies (01/01/2008-10/15/2017). The final sample for multivariate analysis includes 4,651 financing event observations from 3,250 ventures. Fund inflow data was obtained from Preqin.
method: Quantitative empirical study using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regressions for venture valuations and Logit regressions for long-term investment success. Twitter sentiment was measured using the VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner) lexicon and rule-based method. The study examined direct effects and moderation effects of venture age, VC firm experience, and the interaction between Twitter sentiment and patent applications using interaction terms. Chow tests were used to compare standardized coefficients for signal strength differences.
results: Both Twitter sentiment (a weak signal) and patent applications (a strong signal) are positively associated with venture valuations, but patents have a significantly stronger effect (H1 supported). Patent applications are positively associated with long-term investment success (IPO or acquisition), whereas Twitter sentiment is not (H2 supported). Contrary to expectations, patent applications enhance the signaling value of Twitter sentiment on venture valuations, suggesting a complementarity effect (H3 not supported). Both Twitter sentiment's and patent applications' effects on valuations are stronger for younger ventures (H4a, H4b supported). VC firm experience does not moderate Twitter sentiment's effect on valuations (H5a not supported), but patent applications' effect is stronger for less-experienced VCs (H5b supported). Overall, weak signals like Twitter sentiment might bias VCs’ decisions, leading to potential over-valuations for companies that do not offer higher odds of success.

results: The study proposes a three-step signal interpretation process for investors: (i) extraction of a fundamental signal, (ii) orchestration of other signals, and (iii) scrutiny of consistency among signals. tech_capa_definition: Technological capabilities are implicitly conceptualized as a firm's innovative efforts and technical prowess, the ability to combine knowledge, the caliber of technological advancements, and technical competency, which are indicated by the tangible results of the innovation process (patents).
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized by 'Patent Stock', which is measured as the total number of patent applications filed by the company in the last 4 years prior to the IPO (PAT4) and in the last 5 years prior to the IPO (PAT5).
funding_success_definition: Funding success is conceptualized as the success of an IPO from the company’s perspective, specifically referring to the amount of capital collected at IPO.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized as 'IPO performance' and measured by 'Proceeds (PROCEED)', which is the amount of money raised by a firm via an IPO. It is computed by multiplying the number of total issues by the firm’s issue price on the IPO day.
signal_vehicles: patent application
mediation_moderation_effects: The paper explicitly examines and finds that industry technological domain (high-tech vs. low-tech) moderates the signaling effect of patents on IPO performance. The interaction impact between the high-tech dummy and the quantity of patent applications before IPO is significantly negative to the amount of cash generated at IPO.
data: The study utilizes a dataset of 338 newly listed Japanese IPO firms (242 high-tech, 96 low-tech) from Japan Exchange Group (JPX) listed between 1 January 2000 and 31 December 2015. Patenting data were hand-collected from the Japan Platform for Patent Information (JplatPat). IPO data (Proceed, Underwriter, Stock Market) were obtained from Thomson Reuters Eikon. Pre-IPO performance characteristics (number of employees, debt-to-equity ratio, firm age) were extracted from the FinancialQuest database. Firms were classified into high-tech and low-tech using the OECD industrial classification (main analysis) and Thomson Reuters industrial classification (robustness check). The unit of analysis is the firm.
method: The research design employs an empirical analysis using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression models to test the hypotheses.
results: The study found a robust and positive association between the number of patents before an IPO and the amount of cash raised during the IPO (H1 supported). However, for Japanese companies belonging to the high-tech industry sector, patenting activities fail to have a positive signal for the IPO, while low-tech industry sector firms tend to have better IPO performance (H2 supported). Specifically, the interaction effect between the high-tech dummy and the number of patent applications prior to an IPO is significantly negative to the capital collected at IPO. Control variables show that larger companies and reputable underwriters are positively associated with IPO performance, while firm age is negatively associated, and venture capital backing has no significant impact.

Control variables such as 'Patents' (number of patents in a region) and 'Citations' (citations to a region's patents) are also used to measure regional technology resources and success.
funding_success_definition: The paper does not explicitly define 'funding success' as a standalone construct for ventures. It discusses the 'success' of crowdfunding campaigns as an observable attribute that signals quality and legitimacy, and as a mechanism that contributes to subsequent entrepreneurial firm starts.
funding_success_operationalization: For crowdfunding campaigns, 'Successful KS campaigns' is operationalized as a count of Kickstarter projects in specified categories (games, food, technology, fashion, crafts, journalism) that reached their fundraising goals and actually received funds. For the entrepreneurial ventures themselves, the outcome variable is 'New firms' listed in Crunchbase, representing high technology and high growth firm starts.

mediation_moderation_effects: The paper finds that the type of region moderates the effectiveness of crowdfunding campaigns. This indicates that geographical and socio-economic characteristics (urban vs. non-urban, poor vs. wealthy) moderate the impact of CF campaigns on new firm formation.

data: The study uses a county-year panel dataset for U.S. counties from 2009 to 2018 (business registration data ends in 2016), with a subsequent exploratory analysis at the zip code-year level (2011-2016). Data sources include: Kickstarter (scraped data on campaigns), Crunchbase (high technology and high growth firm starts, angel funding, VC funding), Startup Cartography Project (business registration data), U.S. Census (economic and demographic characteristics, population, median household income), County Business Patterns (number of existing firms with <10 employees), and Balsmeier et al. (2018) (patent and citation data). The unit of analysis is county-year for the main analysis and zip code-year for exploratory analysis.
method: The research employs an instrumental variables (IV) regression approach with FIPS (county/zip code) and Year fixed effects to establish causality. Two instruments are used for Kickstarter campaigns: 1) the count of campaigns in 'excluded' categories (film and video, music, comics, dance), and 2) the count of comments on these 'excluded' category campaigns. OLS regressions are also presented for comparison. For the zip code-level analysis, cluster analysis is performed to categorize regions based on demographic and economic characteristics.
results: The study finds a positive and highly significant causal effect of regional Kickstarter crowdfunding campaigns on subsequent 'high technology and high growth entrepreneurship', as measured by new Crunchbase firm listings. One year after campaigns, elasticities range from 0.15 to 0.24. Conversely, crowdfunding campaigns are associated with a slight decrease (2-5%) in more conventional business registrations, suggesting a shift in entrepreneurial focus. These findings are robust when excluding major entrepreneurial hubs. While crowdfunding activity is concentrated in urban, educated, and higher human capital regions, the impact per campaign on new firm formation is greater in less urban and poorer regions (e.g., college towns, diversified/tourism rural, poor/mostly rural). The paper concludes that crowdfunding shifts regional entrepreneurship towards higher-tech, higher-growth ventures.

tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized through information signals related to the product’s technology and legal protection/IPR (Intellectual Property Right).
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized as "investment decisions" (investors' predictions of new venture performance) and "actual performance" of new ventures, which encompasses financial and non-financial parameters delivered after the beginning of operations.
signal_vehicles: patent, trademark
mediation_moderation_effects: Investor’s motivation and ability moderate the perceived usefulness of information in their decision process.
data: A meta-analysis of 75 empirical studies published between 2000 and 2020. The studies focused on investors’ consideration of qualitative information signals while appraising new venture proposals and new-venture performance. The data extracted from primary studies included Pearson’s correlation coefficients and sample sizes. The unit of analysis is the relationship between various information signals and investment decisions or actual new venture performance.
method: The research design is a meta-analysis using a random-effect model, theoretically guided by the Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM). The analytical approach involved calculating Fisher transformation of correlation coefficients, testing homogeneity using the Q-statistic, and determining overall effect sizes.
results: The study found a significant presence of bias in investors' consideration of qualitative information signals when making investment decisions for new ventures, compared to the actual performance of these ventures. The direction and scale of this bias are determined by investor motivation and ability to process information. Investors exhibit a negative bias towards information signals that are difficult to assess (e.g., IPR, social capital, sustainability, macroeconomic factors) or not directly associated with the new venture. Conversely, they show a positive bias towards information signals that are easy to understand and highly relevant (e.g., industry experience, product technology, market potential). Specifically, signals suggesting growth potential (product technology, market potential) are preferred over those suggesting financial risk coverage (experience). For technological capabilities, IPR (including patents and trademarks) was found to have a significant positive effect on both investment decisions and actual performance, but investors were negatively biased towards it, undervaluing its actual role.

signal_vehicles: tweets

AI_venture: The paper examines the emerging Artificial Intelligence (AI) ecosystem in Edmonton, Canada. It finds that in this nascent context, firms employing 'near bridging discursive strategies' through tweets (communicating across related topic clusters like AI Digital and Tech Entrepreneurship) are associated with better positioning in the material network and received additional funding from the provincial government. Conversely, 'distant bridging discursive strategies' were associated with low material presence or venture failure. The study highlights the importance of cultural understandings and discursive practices as precursors to material outcomes in emerging AI entrepreneurial ecosystems, especially for policy attention and government funding.
data: Cultural data from 23,532 tweets from 135 Twitter accounts (AI new ventures + followers of 4+ AI startups) over two years, identified using Crunchbase and local media mentions in the Edmonton AI entrepreneurial ecosystem. Material data was collected from composite maps built from reports by four local experts (director of a local service organization for entrepreneurship, head of a health-based AI firm, government service lead, prominent local investor), detailing tangible and intangible resource ties among actors. The unit of analysis is the regional emerging AI entrepreneurial ecosystem and its constituent actors.
method: The study employs an interpretive data science approach using a three-stage rendering method (corpus compilation, mixed methods analysis, theorizing artifacts). Cultural analysis involved network analysis (Gephi) of bi-directional Twitter followers and topic modeling using Bi-Term Topic Model (BTM) on the tweet corpus to identify discourse clusters, cultural holes, and discursive strategies. Material analysis used social network analysis (UCINET) on expert-drawn maps to measure centrality, constraint, and structural holes. The research design involves a comparative typology contrasting cultural and material ecosystem characteristics.
results: The paper develops a novel cultural mapping technique identifying 'cultural holes' as entrepreneurial possibilities in emerging AI entrepreneurial ecosystems. It finds that discursive strategies, particularly 'near bridging' through tweets about AI/ML SciTech and Tech Entrepreneurship, are correlated with better material positioning and additional government funding for firms like AltaML and Amii. 'Distant bridging' strategies were associated with low material presence or venture failure. The study highlights the importance of cultural understanding and discourse (buzz, mindshare) as precursors to material outcomes in EEEs, suggesting a 'stereoscopic' cultural and material approach for policy analytics over traditional material metrics.

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes technological capabilities as the ability of a start-up to innovate, develop, and deploy new technologies, products, or services. This is reflected in the impetus of technological innovation for new companies, the technical abilities required for patents, the development of technological competences through alliances, and the feasibility of proposed technology as understood by expert CEOs.
tech_capa_operationalization: The paper operationalizes or measures technological capabilities through observable signals such as the number of patents owned, the management team's education, the management team’s international experience, the previous venture creation of the CEO/founder, the technical and scientific expertise of the management team, and the presence of a finished product (beyond the prototype phase).
funding_success_definition: The paper does not explicitly define 'funding success.' It is implicitly understood as the outcome of receiving external investment from venture capitalists.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized as 'financing, that is, how much the venture capital funds have funded every start-up' and the 'likelihood of receiving external investment from VCs'.
signal_vehicles: management education, management team’s international experience, number of patents, previous venture creation of the CEO/founder, strategic alliances, finished product
signal_vehicle_category: human_capital, human_capital, appropriability, human_capital, feasibility, feasibility
signal_effectiveness_direction: effective, effective, effective, effective, ineffective, effective
data: The study uses a dataset collected between 2012 and 2017, composed of 150 observations representing the Italian venture capital market. The data was built on information from the Bureau van Dijk database, supplemented with data collected through a survey sent to VC funds. The unit of analysis is start-ups (new companies). The survey covered human capital of the CEO and management team (education, previous experience), patents held, and strategic alliances.
method: The research design is empirical and quantitative, using an Ordinary Least Squares multiple regression model to estimate relationships between variables. 'Financing' (amount funded by VCs) was the outcome variable, and 'education', 'track record', 'number of patents owned', and 'alliances' were predictor variables.
results: The study found a positive relationship between management education, the management team’s international experience, previous venture creation of the CEO/founder, and the number of patents owned, and the likelihood of receiving financing from VCs. Strategic alliances did not show a statistically significant positive relationship with funding likelihood, mainly due to a small number of start-ups having established them. The study also concludes that owning a finished product (far from the prototype phase) is a clear signal of viability. It highlighted that Italian VCs tend to prefer investing in start-ups at a more mature stage, indicating a cautious, risk-averse approach compared to American VCs.

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes technological capabilities as the 'unobserved quality of young, innovative firms' and the 'technical value or the strength of protection of the underlying inventions.' Specifically, it focuses on 'radical inventions,' defined as inventions 'based on knowledge components that had previously not been combined' and having 'the potential to influence future technical developments' and 'result in a notable advance in the performance of a technology.'
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized by identifying 'patents protecting radical inventions.' An invention is considered radical if any documents of a DOCDB patent family contain at least one pair of International Patent Classification (IPC) classes (main group level seven-digit) that had not been combined in previous patent applications. The study uses a dummy variable, 'DRadical Patent,' which equals 1 for firms owning at least one such patent family at the time of a given VC round. The paper also controls for 'DIncremental Patent' (at least one patent, but no radical inventions) and 'Forward Citations (ln+1)' as a measure of technological impact.
funding_success_definition: Funding success is conceptualized as a firm's ability to acquire external financing, specifically venture capital (VC) funding. It is framed as the 'attractiveness of a firm' for VC investors and the 'likelihood of a deal between a VC investor and a young firm.'
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized as the formation of an investment tie between a firm and a VC investor. This is measured by two dependent variables: 'Realized Investment Tie' (a dummy variable for 1=realized tie, 0=unrealized tie) and 'Dealtype' (a categorical variable for 0=unrealized tie, 1=standalone realized investment tie, 2=syndicated realized investment tie).
signal_vehicles: patent (radical invention)
mediation_moderation_effects: The paper explicitly examines the moderation effect of 'VC investor reputation' ('DReputable Investor') on the relationship between 'patents protecting radical inventions' and the 'likelihood of forming an investment tie' (Hypothesis 1). It also examines how this relationship is further moderated by the 'financing stage' ('DFirst Round'), hypothesizing that effects are weaker in follow-on rounds (Hypothesis 3 and 4). Additionally, syndication is investigated as a mechanism through which reputable VC investors deal with the risk (Hypothesis 2).
industry_sector: life sciences, biotechnology, medical instruments, pharmaceuticals
data: The study uses data from the VICO 4.0 dataset, PATSTAT (European Patent Office), and Thomson Reuters EIKON. The sample consists of 759 young life science firms and 555 independent VC investors located in 27 European countries, the UK, and Israel. The investment period analyzed is 2003–2015. The unit of analysis is the firm-investor dyad, resulting in 148,204 potential firm-investor dyads (of which 1792 were realized ties). Additional data on entrepreneurial team human capital was collected manually for 75.1% of sample firms.
method: The research employs a matching model to compare realized VC investment dyads against unrealized ties. The empirical strategy primarily uses logistic regression for binary outcomes ('Realized Investment Tie') and multinomial logistic regression for categorical outcomes ('Dealtype'). Conditional logit models are used as a robustness check to control for unobserved time-invariant firm characteristics. Average marginal effects (AMEs) are calculated to interpret probabilities.
results: The study finds strong support for its hypotheses. First, in first-round VC investments, young firms with patents protecting radical inventions are more likely to form investment ties with reputable VC investors than firms without such patents (Hypothesis 1 supported). Second, these investment ties between reputable VC investors and firms with radical patents are significantly more likely to be syndicated than standalone (Hypothesis 2 supported). Third, these positive associations (likelihood of tie and syndication) are confined to first-round investments and completely vanish in follow-on rounds, suggesting that the informational value of radical patents diminishes over time as information asymmetry is reduced (Hypotheses 3 and 4 supported, as the effect is not just weaker but disappears). The paper concludes that reputable VC investors are better able to deal with the 'dark side' (high uncertainty and costs) of radical invention signals, primarily through syndication.

tech_capa_operationalization: Growth in number of employees (headcount), which is shown to be positively associated with changes in company equity valuation (postmoney valuation).
funding_success_operationalization: Presence of venture capital (VC) funding (a dummy variable for receiving funding) and the amount of funds raised in a VC round.
signal_vehicles: VC funding event
mediation_moderation_effects: The amount of funds raised in a VC round moderates the intensity and relevance of the VC funding signal on employee growth.
industry_sector: technology, communications_and_networking, electronics_and_computer_hardware, semiconductors, software, information_services, healthcare_and_biotechnology, business_and_consumer_services_and_products
data: Monthly headcount data for 494 startups (193 venture-backed, 301 non-venture-backed) from Trinet, a professional employer organization (PEO), and venture financing data (founding date, funding dates and amounts, postmoney valuation, liquidity events) from VentureOne (Reuters) and Venture Source. The data covers the period 1994–2000, primarily from Silicon Valley-based technology companies. The unit of analysis is startup firms over time.
method: The study employs a signaling theory framework. It uses an event study research design to analyze employee growth around VC funding events, controlling for age, size, previous month's growth, and industry dummies. Regression analysis is used, including interactions for the amount of funds raised. A logit model is used to test if prior growth predicts future VC funding. Spearman’s rank correlation is used to assess the relationship between equity valuation and headcount growth.
results: VC funding events serve as significant signals of startup quality, leading to increased employee growth both prior to and, especially, after the funding event. The intensity of this signal is enhanced by the amount of funds raised. However, prior employee growth does not act as a useful signal for venture capitalists in their funding decisions. A positive and significant association was found between changes in employee headcount and changes in company equity valuation.

title: Venture capital financing and the growth of startup firms

tech_capa_operationalization: total number of patents granted by the European Patent Office (EPO); additionally, includes active patent applications.
funding_success_definition: The premium paid by the private equity (PE) investor during the equity investment, conceptualized as a quantification of the value creation that the investor expects from the deal.
funding_success_operationalization: Calculated as the difference between the Revenue multiple of the deal and the average sector Revenue multiple for acquisitions of stakes superior to 30%, divided by the fair value of the start-up when valued with the sector’s mean multiple. The Revenue multiple is derived from Deal Enterprise Value (EV) disclosed by Mergermarket, divided by the company's revenue in the 12 months prior to the deal.
signal_vehicles: patents, patent applications
mediation_moderation_effects: Previous venture capital (VC) ownership moderates the effect of patents on premium paid; Financial marketing competence moderates the effect of patents on premium paid (and also has an independent positive effect); Private equity (PE) stage-specialization moderates the effect of patents on premium paid.
data: Data on Italian start-ups that underwent a private equity (PE) acquisition (stakes >30%) between 2014 and 2020, collected from Mergermarket, Thomson Financial, and the Italian Camera di Commercio. Patent information was extracted from Delphion (Thomson Financial) for patents issued by the European Patent Office (EPO) with application dates between 2011 and 2019. The sample consists of 237 start-up acquisition deals.
method: Generalized linear model with a logit link and robust clustered error. A Heckman two-stage selection model was used to control for endogeneity, employing the Italian “Budget Law” for innovative start-ups as an instrument. White’s test was used to address heteroskedasticity.
results: Patents alone do not sufficiently generate a higher valuation premium for start-ups from PE investors (ineffective when considered in isolation). However, patents (and patent applications) become effective signals of innovation quality and economic potential when their effect is moderated by specific contingent factors. These contingent factors include: previous venture capital (VC) backing, the financial marketing competence of the financial advisor involved in the deal, and the stage-specialization of the incoming PE investor. Financial marketing competence was also found to have an independent positive effect on the premium paid. Higher profitability (ROA) and stronger sales growth positively impact the premium paid, while R&D expenses alone and firm size do not appear to be influential factors.

Firms in the sample had a headcount of less than approximately 35 and had completed a series A round of VC investment. Respondents were typically founders or senior executive officers. The unit of analysis is the start-up firm.
method: The study employs empirical analysis using univariate comparisons, multivariate Probit models for the likelihood of funding via a direct VC tie, and OLS regressions for pre-money valuation. A log-log empirical specification is used for continuous variables.

tech_capa_definition: Technological capabilities are conceptualized as the firm's ability to deliver value and drive favorable project quality perceptions, contributing to 'technological legitimacy'.
funding_success_operationalization: Primary: Natural log of pledge amount in USD (Pledge). Alternative measures: Ratio of pledge to campaign goal (Pledge effectiveness); Ratio of pledge to duration (Pledge efficiency).
signal_vehicles: project risk description
mediation_moderation_effects: Project location moderates the linear relationship between risk description length and crowdfunding success, such that the positive effect is stronger for projects in developing countries. Project location does not significantly moderate the curvilinear relationships between risk description length/sentiment and crowdfunding success.
data: Secondary data from Kickstarter, collected in 2024 using a third-party web crawler (Octoparse). The sample consists of 1059 technology campaigns launched after September 20, 2012, with 424 projects from 15 developing countries and 635 projects from developed countries. The unit of analysis is the crowdfunding campaign.
method: The study employs signaling theory and curvilinear analysis. Statistical analyses, including OLS regression analysis in SPSS, Haye’s PROCESS (SPSS), and Lind and Mehlum’s utest (STATA), were used to test hierarchical regression models for curvilinear and moderation relationships. Sentiment analysis was performed using the 'sentimentr' package in R.
results: Project risk description length has an inverted U-shaped relationship with crowdfunding success (campaign pledges), with an optimal length around 270 words. Project risk description sentiment has a U-shaped relationship with crowdfunding success (campaign pledges), meaning higher positive sentiment is generally better after a certain minimum threshold. The positive linear effect of risk description length on crowdfunding success is stronger for projects in developing countries. Project location does not significantly moderate the curvilinear effects of risk description length or sentiment on crowdfunding success. Platform endorsement (Kickstarter's 'Projects We Love' badge) is found to be a strong positive driver of crowdfunding success.

title: Saying enough vs saying too much: Lessons on optimizing project risk description for crowdfunding success in developing countries

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes technological capabilities through 'innovative activities' and 'inventive activity', specifically within the context of 'green innovation' or 'green technologies'. It defines 'green innovative activities' as those tracking patents at the firm level that are classified under specific Cooperative Patent Classification (CPC) schemes (Y02 and Y04) related to climate change mitigation technologies.
tech_capa_operationalization:
Technological capabilities, particularly green innovation, are operationalized primarily through patents. Specific measures include: 
- `GreenPat`: a dummy variable equal to one if the firm holds at least one green patent at the year of funding, and zero otherwise.
- `OtherPat`: a dummy variable equal to one if the firm’s patent portfolio contains non-green patents only at the year of funding, and zero otherwise.
- `GreenPatRatio`: a continuous variable measuring the ratio between the number of green patents over the total number of patents a company holds.
- `TotPat`: a continuous variable counting the total number of registered patents (green and non-green).
- `R&D`: a dummy variable taking the value of one if the firm develops any research and development activity, used as a control for attitude towards innovation.
funding_success_definition: The paper implicitly defines funding success as the likelihood or probability of a firm attracting Venture Capital (VC) financing compared to other sources of equity finance.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized using a dummy indicator, `VC`, which takes the value of one if an observed equity transaction is for VC finance, and zero if it is for other types of equity finance. A broader definition, `VC broad`, is also used in robustness checks, including Accelerator, Business Angel, Venture Recapitalization, Venture Leasing, and Corporate Venture Capital.
signal_vehicles: green patent, non-green patent
mediation_moderation_effects:
The paper examines several moderation effects:
- **Investment stage**: No significant differential effect was found for green or non-green patents on the probability of raising later-stage VC investments compared to early-stage ones.
- **Investor type (Corporate VC vs. Independent VC)**: Corporate VCs (CVCs) do not respond in a systematically different manner to green or non-green patents compared to Independent VCs (IVCs).
- **Investor type (Business Angels vs. Independent VC)**: The probability of investing in firms with green or non-green patents is lower for Business Angels (BAs) compared to Independent VCs (IVCs).
- **Geographical dimension**: The relationship between green patenting and VC finance holds across all countries in the sample (EU-27 and UK), but the magnitude of the effect varies by country (e.g., Germany and the UK show higher relevance, while France's effect is reversed in comparison to the average, though still positive).
financing_context: venture_capital, private_equity, equity_crowdfunding, angel_investor, other (Growth Equity, Accelerator, Corporate Venture Capital, Venture Recapitalization, Venture Leasing)
financing_stage: seed, early_stage, later_stage, first_round, second_round, third_round, fourth_round, fifth_round, sixth_round, seventh_round, eighth_round, ninth_round
industry_sector: clean energy technologies, climate change mitigation technologies
data: The study uses a unique matched dataset covering the period 2008–2017, compiled by integrating information from three sources: Dow Jones VentureSource (detailed equity deal information), Orbis (company-level financial and balance sheet data), and Patstat (bibliographical and legal event patent data). The sample comprises companies located in EU-27 and the United Kingdom. The unit of analysis is the single VC or other equity transaction completed.
method: The research employs a probabilistic regression model (Probit model) to analyze the likelihood of receiving VC funding. To address potential endogeneity and systematic heterogeneity, a Propensity Score Matching (PSM) procedure using the Nearest-Neighbor (NN) algorithm was implemented. The study also uses Oster (2019) methodology to test for omitted variable bias and Hansen’s test (Hansen 2000) for non-linearities in patent effects. Various fixed effects, including year, country, sector (NACE2), and interacted sector x country fixed effects, are incorporated to control for unobserved heterogeneity.
results: The study finds that green innovators are significantly more likely to receive Venture Capital (VC) funding compared to other equity financing than firms without green patents. Specifically, having at least one green patent increases the probability of receiving VC finance by approximately 19-20%, while holding non-green patents increases this probability by 12-14%. Green patents are found to be a statistically stronger signal than non-green patents. A larger share of green patents within a firm’s total patent portfolio also increases the probability of attracting VC funds. Robustness checks confirm these findings across alternative measures of green innovation and broader definitions of VC. The impact of green patenting does not significantly differ between early and later investment stages, nor between Corporate VC and Independent VC investors. However, Business Angels are found to be less likely to invest in firms with green or non-green patents compared to Independent VCs. The positive relationship between green patenting and VC finance holds across different European countries, although the magnitude of the effect shows some geographical heterogeneity. The findings underscore that granular, patent-specific green innovation is a crucial driver for VC investment, rather than merely operating in a broadly defined green sector.

title: Venture capital financing and green patenting

funding_success_definition: The entrepreneur securing required funds from an outside investor who breaks even in expectation and is willing to provide immediate and future conditional funding.
funding_success_operationalization: The investor accepting the financial contract and providing funding for experimentation and potential project implementation, contingent on specified performance or information. Empirically, moving from an early development phase (Phase I) to a more capital-intensive phase (Phase II).
signal_vehicles: experimentation duration, performance-sensitive payments, compensation in case of failure, milestones (contingent financing), entrepreneur's initial wealth/internal financing
mediation_moderation_effects: Investment timing distortions decrease with the proportion of high types (q); Investment timing distortions decrease with internal financing (A); Investment timing distortions increase with the relative size of unpledgeable rents (α). Internal financing partly substitutes for investment delay as a signaling instrument.
financing_stage: early_stage, Phase I, Phase II, first-time entrepreneurs
data: A theoretical model of an entrepreneur with private information about a project's profitability (type) requiring outside funding for costly experimentation. The model assumes continuous time, a common discount rate, and a Poisson process for detecting bad projects. The unit of analysis is the optimal financial contract and investment decision for an individual project/entrepreneur.
method: Theoretical modeling using real option theory and signaling games under adverse selection. The study extends the standard real option model and examines optimal financial contracts by solving an optimization program (P) based on Maskin and Tirole (1992) for equilibrium contracts, which incorporates incentive compatibility, participation, and limited liability constraints.
results: Equilibrium financial contracts combine cash flow rights and investment timing to convey private information and separate entrepreneur types. High-type entrepreneurs (with a higher prior probability of a good project) delay investment longer than in the first-best scenario and receive performance-sensitive payments (e.g., vested stock options). Low-type entrepreneurs (or those with riskier projects) may receive compensation even if the project fails (e.g., severance payments, golden parachutes), and invest at the first-best timing. Investment timing distortions are more significant when the adverse selection problem is severe (e.g., lower proportion of high types, higher unpledgeable rents). Internal financing acts as an additional signaling instrument, accelerating investment and reducing timing distortions, but increasing the risk of failure post-investment.

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes technological capabilities as the ability of startups to undertake significant 'technical and resource development,' particularly in their early phases, to demonstrate the 'technical feasibility' of their ideas and develop their 'core service and underlying technologies' in the nascent space economy.
tech_capa_operationalization: The paper measures 'Number of patent applications' filed by startups as the cumulative number of applications over the years, included in models as a natural logarithm. This variable acts as a proxy for innovation output, which is closely related to technological capabilities.
funding_success_definition: The paper implicitly defines funding success as the ability of startups to 'attract Venture Capital funding' and 'access Venture Capital (VC) funding.'
funding_success_operationalization: The paper operationalizes funding success using a dummy variable, 'VC-backing,' which has a value of 1 in the first year a startup receives an investment round from Venture Capitalists (VCs) and 0 otherwise.
signal_vehicles: Participation in ESA BIC program, Patent applications
industry_sector: space, new space economy, upstream, downstream, Earth Observation, Positioning/navigation/timing, Satellite Communications, Science, Space Exploration, Space Technologies, Space Transportation
data: A unique dataset of 1043 European space startups (357 supported by ESA BICs) observed over 2005–2022, forming an unbalanced panel of 7190 firm-year observations. Data sources include Crunchbase, Pitchbook, Orbis (for firm demographics, balance-sheet, and profit-and-loss data), a public list of ESA BIC supported startups, VICO 6.0 (for Venture Capital funding), and Orbis Intellectual Property database (for patent applications). Qualitative data consists of six semi-structured interviews with representatives of ESA BIC programs in Italy and France. The unit of analysis is the space startup (firm-year observations).
method: The study employs Propensity Score Matching (PSM) with iterative matching over years without replacement to create comparable groups of supported and non-supported startups. For startup growth (Hypothesis 1), an augmented Gibrat Law growth model is estimated via fixed-effect panel OLS regression. For the likelihood of receiving VC funding (Hypothesis 2), discrete-time duration models (logistic hazard model and cloglog model specification) are used. The quantitative analysis is complemented by a qualitative exploratory investigation through semi-structured interviews.
results: Supported startups experienced higher growth in total assets both during the program (around 56% additional growth rate) and afterwards (around 37% additional growth rate), and higher growth in employment during the program, but not afterwards. No significant differences were observed in turnover growth. Supported startups have a markedly higher likelihood of attracting Venture Capital funding. Qualitative interviews confirmed that startups prioritize technical and resource development over commercialization in early phases, and the 'brand' of ESA BICs and ESA itself acts as a valuable 'quality signal' for investors. A higher number of patent applications had a positive effect on employees and total assets growth, but not on turnover growth. Startup growth tends to slow down over time.

tech_capa_definition: Technological capabilities are conceptualized as the ability to produce or commercialize cutting-edge technology and R&D capability.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities are operationalized by the receipt of R&D subsidy, patent intensity (total number of patents divided by averaged annual sales in the past three years prior to IPO), and R&D intensity (averaged ratio of R&D expenditures to annual sales in the past three years prior to IPO).
funding_success_definition: Funding success is defined as entrepreneurial firms' Initial Public Offering (IPO) performance, an important milestone for external financing and an early-stage measure for firm ability to acquire substantial resources for growth, reflecting market valuation.
funding_success_operationalization: IPO performance is measured as the mean of two components: Net proceeds from IPO (NetPro), which is the cash received minus IPO costs; and Pre-money market valuation of firms (PreVal), computed as IPO subscription price multiplied by (total shares outstanding minus shares offered). A natural log transformation was applied.
signal_vehicles: receipt of R&D subsidy, receipt of non-R&D subsidy, patent
mediation_moderation_effects: State ownership moderates the inverted U-shape relationship between R&D subsidy and IPO performance, flattening it. Patent intensity moderates the inverted U-shape relationship between R&D subsidy and IPO performance, flattening it. State ownership does not moderate the positive relationship between non-R&D subsidy and IPO performance. Patent intensity does not moderate the positive relationship between non-R&D subsidy and IPO performance.
data: Data from 269 IT entrepreneurial firms in China that went public on the ChiNext Board (Growth Enterprise Board) and SME Board (Small and Medium-Sized Enterprise Board) between 2004 and 2015. Data was compiled from CSMAR (China Stock Market & Accounting Research), WIND (IPO prospectuses), CVSource (China Venture Source), and SIPO (the State Intellectual Property Office of China). The unit of analysis is the IT entrepreneurial firm.
method: OLS regressions with robust standard errors. Robustness checks included Generalized Propensity Score (GPS) estimation and Two-Stage Residual Inclusion (2SRI) estimation using instrumental variables.
results: R&D subsidy has an inverted U-shape effect on IPO performance, meaning performance first increases then decreases with higher subsidy. Non-R&D subsidy has a positive effect on IPO performance. State ownership flattens the inverted U-shape relationship between R&D subsidy and IPO performance. Patent intensity also flattens the inverted U-shape relationship between R&D subsidy and IPO performance. Neither state ownership nor patent intensity moderates the positive relationship between non-R&D subsidy and IPO performance.

tech_capa_definition: The paper conceptualizes technological capabilities implicitly as the industry-specific knowledge and skills founders possess (pre-entry experience) and, crucially, the new venture's capacity to learn from and build upon those endowments. This includes the ability to manage, maintain, and recombine knowledge, particularly technical knowledge inherited or drawn from former employers.
tech_capa_operationalization:
Technological capabilities are operationalized through several measures: 
- **Pre-entry experience**: A dummy variable equal to one if one of the founders worked for an existing firm in the semiconductor industry the year before founding the new venture, and zero otherwise. 
- **Knowledge linkage (proportion)**: Measured as the ratio between the number of backward citations of the new venture’s patents to the former employer and the total number of backward citations. This indicates how much new ventures draw on former employers’ technical knowledge. 
- **Knowledge linkage (count)**: The absolute number of backward citations to former employers (used in robustness checks). 
- **Patent stock**: Sum of the number of patent applications filed each month by the new venture, using a five-year moving sum (as a control for the new venture's knowledge base).
funding_success_definition: Funding success is conceptualized as the ability of new ventures to mobilize financial resources from factor markets, specifically focusing on the speed with which they secure their first round of venture capital funding.
funding_success_operationalization: The dependent variable is 'time it takes for a new venture to receive the first round of VC funding,' measured as the age of the venture in months, calculated from the founding date to the date when the first round of VC funding is secured. A dummy variable 'First VC event' marks the month a venture received its first round of VC funding.
signal_vehicles: founder experience, patent citations
mediation_moderation_effects:
The paper explicitly examines two moderation effects:
1. Knowledge linkages to founders’ former employers moderate the effect of pre-entry experience on the time to first VC funding (Hypothesis 1), strengthening the positive impact of pre-entry experience.
2. Industry clustering moderates the interaction between pre-entry experience and knowledge linkages, such that the amplifying effect of knowledge linkages is stronger when industry clustering is higher (Hypothesis 2).
data: The study uses data on new ventures in the US semiconductor industry between 1997 and 2014. The sample consists of 731 new ventures founded between 1997 and 2009, tracked until 2014. The main list of new ventures is from the Pinestream Consulting Group, augmented with data from ThomsonOne’s VentureXpert, Dow Jones’ Venture Source, Crunchbase, company websites, and LinkedIn for founders' employment histories. Patent and citation histories are from the US Patent and Trademark Office’s PatentsView database. Industry activity data is from the US Bureau of Labor Statistics. Alliance activity is from the Strategic Alliance database of the Securities Data Company (SDC), and government grants are from Small Business Innovation Research data. The unit of analysis is the new venture–month, resulting in 81,381 new venture–month observations.
method: The research design employs event history analysis, specifically using accelerated failure time (AFT) models with a log-normal distribution to model the time to the first VC funding event. A split-sample approach is used for Hypothesis 2 to analyze effects in low versus high industry clustering environments. Robustness checks include alternative measures for knowledge linkages and clustering, and testing with a three-way interaction term instead of split-sample analysis.
results:
1. New ventures with pre-entry experience secure their first round of VC funding faster, experiencing an average 55.8% reduction in time compared to ventures without such experience.
2. Knowledge linkages to founders’ former employers significantly strengthen the effect of pre-entry experience. New ventures with pre-entry experience that draw more on former employers’ knowledge (e.g., 10% knowledge linkage) secure first-round VC funding approximately 35% faster than those without such linkages.
3. The positive impact of knowledge linkages on speeding up VC funding is significantly stronger in environments with high industry clustering. For a new venture with 10% knowledge linkage, the time to first VC funding decreases by about 60% in high clustering areas, compared to a 20% decrease in low clustering areas.

tech_capa_definition: The probability of success of a technology, or the quality of a firm's underlying technology, which can be high (θH) or low (θL).
tech_capa_operationalization: The number of patents filed by a startup (FILED PAT) is used as a signal for the quality of the firm’s underlying technology. The variable READY FOR MKT (technology ready for commercialization or manufacturing feasibility stage) is also used as a control variable for the stage of technology.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized as: (1) a binary variable (VC BINARY) indicating whether a startup received venture capital funding in a given year, and the amount (VC FUNDS) received; (2) a binary variable (ANGEL BINARY) indicating whether a startup received business angel funding in a given year, and the amount (ANGEL FUNDS) received.
signal_vehicles: patents, founder successful startup experience, founder master's degree in science or engineering
signal_effectiveness_direction: mixed (effective for VCs, ineffective for Angels), effective (for VCs), ineffective
mediation_moderation_effects: Investor preferences (venture capitalists vs. business angels) moderate the effectiveness of patents and FFF money as signals. Venture capitalists value patents (signaling technology quality) more highly than founder commitment (signaled by FFF money), while business angels value founder commitment (signaled by FFF money) more highly than patents (signaling technology quality). The theoretical model predicts that the optimal investment in signals (patents vs. FFF money) depends on the external investors' preferences.
industry_sector: technology, information technology, communication, hardware, pharmaceutical, biotechnology, medical devices, microelectronic products, life sciences
data: The study uses a novel dataset of 117 technology startups incubated at the Advanced Technology Development Center (ATDC) of the Georgia Institute of Technology, covering the period 1998–2008, resulting in 471 firm-year observations. Data was gathered from an annual compulsory survey of member companies, augmented with information from 80 startup business plans, 37 founder surveys, and founder websites/LinkedIn profiles. The unit of analysis is the firm-year. The majority of companies (54%) were in the information technology sector.
method: The research combines a theoretical model of startup signaling with an empirical analysis using two-stage least squares (2SLS) regression models, including linear probability models for binary outcomes. This approach accounts for the endogeneity of signals (patents and FFF money) with respect to external funding. Instruments used include founder relational ties (CLOSENESS), number of founders with a PhD in science or engineering (PHD), and time spent at ATDC (CYCLE). Robustness checks were performed by re-estimating models excluding outliers.
results: The study finds that patents and FFF money are jointly endogenous to venture capital and business angel investment. The number of patents filed has a large, positive, and statistically significant impact on both the likelihood (46% increase for a 1% increase in patents) and amount of venture capital funding. In contrast, FFF money does not significantly impact venture capital funding. Conversely, FFF money has a positive and statistically significant impact on both the likelihood (5% increase for a 1% increase in FFF money) and amount of business angel funding, while patents do not significantly impact business angel funding. This suggests venture capitalists value technology quality (signaled by patents) more than founder commitment (signaled by FFF money), and the reverse for business angels. The impact of patents on venture capital investment is found to be stronger than the impact of FFF money on business angel investment. Founder successful startup experience and commercializing proprietary knowledge from Georgia Institute of Technology also positively affect venture capital investment.

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes technological capabilities in terms of a firm's innovativeness, commercial potential, and its capacity for research and development.
