tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes technological capabilities through the concept of 'technology endowment', defined by the fundamental innovative features of licensed patents, including originality, closeness to science, and generality of research outcomes. This endowment is part of the 'unobservable quality' of a spinoff, which refers to its long-term ability to generate revenues by effectively transferring and commercializing research results into marketable products/services.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technology endowment for spinoff companies is measured using measures of patent characteristics: Patent generality (Herfindahl index on technological classes of citing patents), Patent originality (Herfindahl index on technological classes of cited patents), and Patent scientific base (measure of the scientific base outside the patent system). These measures are used to indicate a patent’s impact on later innovation in a field and its fundamental innovative features.
funding_success_definition: Funding success is conceptualized as the ability of academic spinoffs to obtain financing from outside investors, particularly venture capital (VC) funding, which is essential for their long-term viability and future market success (e.g., sales growth).
funding_success_operationalization: Follow-on venture capital (VC) funding is operationalized using a binary variable (coded 1 if the spinoff received any venture support, 0 otherwise) and the inverse hyperbolic sine (IHS) transformation of the amount of venture capital obtained in dollars. The spinoffs' economic performance, as an outcome of successful funding, is measured by the IHS transformation of sales growth between 2007 and 2010 in dollars.
signal_vehicles: TTO gap funds, Patent generality, Patent originality, Patent scientific base, Number of founders, Education, Work experience, Previous experience with VC, Previous venture experience
signal_vehicle_category: feasibility, feasibility, feasibility, feasibility, human_capital, human_capital, human_capital, human_capital, human_capital
signal_effectiveness_direction: effective, ineffective, effective, effective, ineffective, ineffective, ineffective, effective, ineffective
mediation_moderation_effects: The paper finds an indirect positive effect of university commercialization funds (TTO gap funds) on spinoff companies’ sales growth, mediated by follow-on venture capital financing (TTO gap funding --> VC financing --> Sales growth).
data: The study uses a database of 112 spinoff firms established to exploit inventions assigned to the University of Michigan (U-M) between 1999 and 2010. Data sources include the U-M TTO's database, USPTO database for patent information, VenturXpert, Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA), SBA TECH-Net database, and Orbis database for sales and employee data. The unit of analysis is the spinoff firm.
method: The research employs logit models to estimate the likelihood of receiving venture capital (VC) financing, tobit models to estimate the amount of VC funding received (due to left-censored data with many zero values), and OLS regressions for analyzing spinoffs' sales growth. The Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney test is used for non-parametric comparisons of sales growth distributions. Mediation analysis follows Baron and Kenny's (1986) framework, including Sobel tests where appropriate for indirect effects.
results: University commercialization funds (TTO gap funds) positively and significantly increase both the likelihood and the amount of venture capital (VC) financing received by spinoff companies (Hypotheses 1 and 2 supported). While TTO gap funds do not have a direct significant influence on spinoffs' sales growth when VC financing is considered, they have an indirect positive effect on sales growth, mediated by follow-on VC investments (Hypothesis 3 partially supported through mediation). Patent originality and scientific base are found to have a positive and significant effect on attracting VC funding. In contrast, patent generality, the number of founders, founders' education (found to have a negative effect on VC amount), and founders' work experience do not significantly influence VC decisions. Founders' previous experience with VC has a significant positive impact on attracting VC funding, while previous venture experience does not have a statistically significant effect.

funding_success_definition: Companies with an initial public offering (IPO) or a trade sale are classified as successful.
funding_success_operationalization: time to successful exit, time to IPO (precluding private exit), time to private exit (precluding IPO), multinomial logit regression with four categories of exits (IPOs, trade sales, secondary sales, write-offs) and nonexits as a reference category.
signal_vehicles: venture lending financing choice, venture capital financing choice
industry_sector: Business and financial services, Consumer goods, Consumer services, Energy and utilities, Healthcare, Industrial goods and materials, Information technology
data: An extensive novel data set of 50,623 debt and equity financing rounds in 12,629 U.S. VC-backed companies founded between 1995 and 2008, tracked until 2013. Data sources include Dow Jones Venture Source, Standard & Poor’s (S&P) Capital IQ, Bureau van Dijk Orbis, National Venture Capital Association (NVCA), and Thomson ONE. The unit of analysis is at the company and round level.
method: Theoretical model (sequential Bayesian game) to examine financing decisions under perfect and asymmetric information. Empirical tests use logit regressions on the round level, Cox (1972) proportional hazard models, Cox model with competing risks (Fine and Gray (1999)), and multinomial logit regression on the company level.
results: Theoretically, under perfect information, high-quality companies obtain VC financing, and there is no VL. Under asymmetric information, early-stage VC investors may strictly prefer VL to VC. Early-stage VC investors that finance high-quality value companies (low upside potential, high liquidation value) tend to prefer VL in the late stage and signal their company type. For growth companies (high upside potential), VC is preferred. VC financing is also preferred when late-stage VC investors can add considerable value or when uncertainty is high. The existence of VL increases the number of positive-value projects and the amount of early-stage VC financing. Empirically, a low proportion of high-quality companies, older company age, a high-ranking VC in previous rounds, and higher liquidation value increase the likelihood of obtaining VL. High uncertainty is negatively related to the use of VL. However, VL is significantly and negatively related to successful exits in general (IPO, trade sale, secondary sale), concluding that VL is not associated with better outcomes.

data: VC investments data from PE Daily database (PEDaily.cn), containing 7337 cases from 1992 (searched Jan 1, 2017). Patent data from 'Patent Search and Analysis Platform' of State Intellectual Property Office of China (SIPO). The study focused on 57 bio-pharmaceutical companies with 63 investment cases. Unit of analysis: investment events and companies.
method: Descriptive analysis was used to profile overall VC investments in China. An exploratory multi-case study approach was used for the bio-pharmaceutical industry, analyzing the interaction between VC and patent activities in four typical companies.
results: The Chinese VC market has flourished since 2003, with IT/ICT/Electronic, Consumer goods/Manufacture, and Biology/Pharmaceuticals/Chemicals being the top attractive industries. The study finds a virtuous circle where patents positively affect VC financing, and VC funding, in turn, accelerates a company’s patenting speed. Companies with strong technical bases and market share can easily attract VC, using the funds for R&D or acquisitions to enhance competitiveness. Alignment of patent IPC distribution with strategic business goals is important for financing. An international R&D background (indicated by patent families) is conducive to securing VC. The interactions between VC investment and patents can vary; strong leadership or promising industry prospects can lead to VC funding even with fewer initial patents. In China, government policy support and the origin of a company from state-owned enterprises or national institutions can facilitate easier access to VC, potentially reducing the emphasis on patents.

tech_capa_definition: The paper does not explicitly define 'technological capabilities'. However, it defines 'absorptive capacity' as 'a firm’s capability to recognize the value of new knowledge, assimilate and exploit it' based on its previous knowledge. It further distinguishes between 'potential absorptive capacity' (capability to understand and capture new knowledge) and 'realized absorptive capacity' (ability to internalize and implement absorbed knowledge). The paper states that firms with high potential absorptive capacity 'would better control and build their ability to extend the scope of knowledge and technology'.
tech_capa_operationalization: Potential absorptive capacity was measured as the 'development costs up to IPO' (referred to as R&D expense in the results section). Realized absorptive capacity was observed based on the 'number of patents held by the startups at the time of IPO'.
funding_success_definition: Not explicitly defined. However, it is implicitly conceptualized as a startup's 'sustainable growth and performance' and 'high performance against market expectations'.
funding_success_operationalization: Simplified Tobin’s Q, calculated as the market value of a startup divided by its net asset value. A robustness check also used 'underpricing', which is a method that undervalues the actual value of the startups based on the stock price on the day of IPO.
mediation_moderation_effects: Potential absorptive capacity positively moderated the association between the initial VC investment round and startup performance. Realized absorptive capacity did not show significant moderating effects. VC reputation did not show a moderating effect on the relationship between initial VC investment and startup performance.
data: The study utilized a sample of 363 IPO firms from the United States, listed between 2000 and 2007. Data on initial VC investments were collected from Thomson One. Compustat data from Wharton Data Research Service (WRDS) were used to observe post-IPO performance, negotiation power, and absorptive capacity. The unit of analysis is IPO firms.
method: The research design involved examining the main effects of initial VC investment timing on startup performance, and the moderating effects of absorptive capacity and VC reputation. This was analyzed using regression models.
results: The study found that the earlier a startup obtains initial VC investment, the higher its performance (Hypothesis 1 weakly supported). Potential absorptive capacity positively moderated this association, meaning that firms with high potential absorptive capacity achieved higher performance if VC investment occurred at an early stage (Hypothesis 2a supported). Realized absorptive capacity did not show significant moderating effects (Hypothesis 2b rejected). The reputation of VC companies also did not show a moderating effect on startup performance (Hypothesis 3 rejected). A robustness check indicated that startups securing early VC investment tended to engage in underpricing more than others. A supplementary analysis showed that potential absorptive capacity directly improved startup performance, while realized absorptive capacity had no significant direct effect.

funding_success_definition: The probability of an angel-backed company raising follow-on venture capital (VC) financing.
funding_success_operationalization: A step dummy variable, _VC_invested_, which takes value 1 starting from the year in which the company received the first VC investment, and 0 otherwise.
mediation_moderation_effects: BAN membership moderates the relationship between BAs’ rejection rate and the probability of receiving follow-on VC funding, such that the positive effect of rejection rate is stronger for non-BAN members. BAN membership moderates the relationship between BAs’ active monitoring and the probability of receiving follow-on VC funding, such that the negative effect of monitoring is stronger for BAN members.
data: Dataset of Italian angel-backed companies from IBAN survey waves (2008-2016), matched with Bureau Van Dijk Orbis, Crunchbase, Zephyr, and Private Equity Monitor databases. The final sample consists of 176 unique companies (673 firm-year observations) that received a first angel investment round, observed over the period 2009-2018. The unit of analysis is firm-year observation.
method: Panel logit models, with robustness checks using separate panel logit models for sub-samples.
results: Companies financed by Business Angels (BAs) with higher rejection rates and BAs affiliated with an angel network (BAN) have a higher probability of receiving follow-on Venture Capital (VC) funding. A high level of BA monitoring activity negatively influences the probability of obtaining later VC funding. The positive impact of BAs’ rejection rate on later VC funding is stronger for non-BAN members; for BAN members, the rejection rate does not have a significant effect. A high level of monitoring by BAs conveys a negative signal for VCs specifically if the BA is affiliated with a BAN; for non-BAN members, monitoring has a non-significant role. Higher capital invested by BAs and larger syndication in BA investments also favor follow-on VC financing, as does BA investing experience.

tech_capa_operationalization: Technological novelty of patents, measured by the inverse standardized combination frequency of Cooperative Patent Classification at the subclass and subgroup levels. The firm-level value is the lowest (most novel) among its patents. The proportion of novel combinations at the subgroup level is also used. The number of patents is used as a control variable for innovation.
funding_success_definition: IPO speed, which is defined as the number of years from the founding of a start-up until it goes public. It reflects the quality of a high-tech start-up and is positively related to the investment return for early-stage investors, and is considered a performance milestone and financial success.
funding_success_operationalization: Measured in years from the time the start-up was formed to the time it went public. Start-ups that remained private or exited through acquisition during the observation period (up to 10 years) were considered failed IPOs for this measure.
signal_vehicles: patent (novelty), patent (conventionality), founder inventor, premium VC backing, number of patents
mediation_moderation_effects: Conventionality of invention moderates the negative relationship between technological novelty of inventions and IPO speed. Founder inventor status moderates the negative relationship between technological novelty of inventions and IPO speed. Premium VC investor backing moderates the negative relationship between technological novelty of inventions and IPO speed.
data: Sample of 408 VC-backed healthcare start-ups (biotech and medical device) founded between 1990 and 2010 in the US and Canada. Data collected from Crunchbase, USPTO, Harvard Patent Disambiguated Assignee Dataset, Pitchbook, LinkedIn, Bloomberg, company/university websites, Thomson Reuters SDC Platinum, and BioCentury. Unit of analysis is the start-up firm.
method: Survival analysis using Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) models with log-logistic distribution (primary) and Weibull distribution (robustness testing). Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) models for robustness tests. Poisson regression models and two-stage least-squares (2SLS) analysis with instrumental variables (average novelty of start-ups in the same state/industry) for endogeneity testing.
results: The technological novelty of inventions hinders the IPO process of high-tech start-ups, as late-stage public investors perceive novel technology as high-risk, leading to slower IPO speed. Specifically, start-ups with novel inventions take double the time to go public on average compared to those with non-novel inventions. This negative effect is mitigated when the invention is also characterized by conventionality, when the founder is also the inventor, or when the start-up is backed by premium VC investors. The amount of VC financing and support by premium VC, and earlier VC financing, effectively reduce the time to IPO. Conventionality of invention by itself is positively related to time to IPO. Total number of patents has a positive effect on IPO performance and speed.

tech_capa_definition: Digital entrepreneurship is conceptualized as a new form of entrepreneurial activity in which part or all of the entrepreneurial process has been digitized or other information and communication technologies are used to pursue opportunities. This implicitly reflects a firm's utilization of digital technologies and serves as a signal of entrepreneurial quality to investors.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technological capabilities, in the form of 'digital entrepreneurship', are operationalized by counting the number of internet technology uses by firms from a list of eight activities: (1) setting up a corporate website; (2) opening an online store; (3) advertising and promoting the business; (4) recruiting talents; (5) collaborating with other Internet firms; (6) setting up a corporate Weibo and WeChat official account; (7) investing in search engine bidding rankings; and (8) communicating with and servicing customers. This count is then log-transformed.
funding_success_definition: Implicitly defined as the ability of entrepreneurial firms to obtain more external venture capital to support further expansion and growth, and to increase their market value.
funding_success_operationalization: Measured as the logarithm of the share of total loans in the entrepreneurial firms’ working capital.
signal_vehicles: corporate website, online store, digital advertising, collaboration with other Internet firms, corporate Weibo account, corporate WeChat official account, search engine bidding rankings investment, digital customer communication and service
signal_vehicle_category: feasibility, feasibility, feasibility, feasibility, feasibility, feasibility, feasibility, feasibility
signal_effectiveness_direction: effective, effective, effective, effective, effective, effective, effective, effective
mediation_moderation_effects: The paper examined the moderating effect of entrepreneurs’ socioeconomic status on the positive relationship between digital entrepreneurship and venture capital, but found it to be negative and statistically insignificant.
data: Firm-level cross-sectional data from a 2016 questionnaire survey of Chinese private entrepreneurial firms, established less than 10 years ago and with fewer than 500 employees in 2015. The survey was conducted by the United Front Work Department of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party, the All-China Federation of Industry & Commerce, the State Administration for Industry and Commerce, and the China Private Economy Association. Regional-level data (economic development, Internet penetration) were obtained from regional statistical yearbooks. The sample included 2,322 observations for the first stage and 907 for the second stage.
method: Ordinary least squares (OLS) regression with industry and province fixed effects was used for the first stage (predicting digital entrepreneurship). Heckman’s two-stage model was employed for the second stage (predicting venture capital) to correct for sample selection bias regarding entrepreneurs having an Internet-era imprint; this involved a logit model for the first stage of the Heckman model and OLS for the second stage. Robustness checks included ordered logistic regression and Tobit models.
results: Entrepreneurs with an Internet-era imprint significantly increase the level of digital entrepreneurship (Hypothesis 1 supported). A higher level of digital entrepreneurship significantly helps entrepreneurial firms access more venture capital (Hypothesis 2 supported). However, the entrepreneurs’ socioeconomic status did not significantly enhance the positive relationship between digital entrepreneurship and venture capital (Hypothesis 3 not supported), with the interaction term being negative and insignificant.

data: A corpus of 5099 company self-descriptions from active small sustainable technology ventures founded between 2005 and 2022, extracted from Crunchbase, covering 19 sustainability-related industries. The unit of analysis is company descriptions.
method: Topic modeling (Latent Dirichlet Allocation and Structural Topic Models) was used to identify latent signals from company self-descriptions. Regression models analyzed the marginal effects of company characteristics (year of incorporation, funding amount, funding round, number of employees) on topic prevalence. results: The study extracted 17 distinct latent signals from company self-descriptions, categorized as task signals (e.g., solar energy, energy efficiency, fuel technology, battery storage) and institutional signals (e.g., analytics, market operations). For instance, energy efficiency, battery storage, fuel technology, and analytics signals increased with larger funding amounts, while organic farming signals decreased. The study also found correlations between different signals, suggesting that consistent, multiple quality signals enhance effectiveness in attracting investment.

tech_capa_operationalization: Project categories (Art & Craft, Design-based, Technology-intensive) classified by innovation difficulty coefficient (kL, kM, kH), which reflects R&D investments, engineering cycles, technical complexity, and capital required to overcome technical barriers and ensure reliability.
funding_success_operationalization: Crowdfunding success (CS) measured as raised fund (RF) minus funding target (FT), and logarithmically transformed (ln_Y1, ln_Y2). Backer engagement measured as the number of pledged investors (PI), logarithmically transformed (ln_Y3, ln_Y4).
signal_vehicles: textual descriptions, product prototypes (images or videos)
mediation_moderation_effects: Project category (Art & Craft, Design-based, Technology-intensive) moderates the impact of project creativity on crowdfunding success. Investors' estimated return (high vs. low) moderates the impact of project creativity on crowdfunding success. Crowdfunding mechanism (Equity AON, Equity KIA, Debt AON, Debt KIA) moderates the impact of project creativity on crowdfunding success.
industry_sector: creative economy, art & craft, design, technology, robotics, pharmaceuticals
data: Dataset of 1332 crowdfunding projects from Wefunder, Kickstarter, and Indiegogo, collected from October 1, 2023 to October 1, 2025. Data includes funding target (FT), raised fund (RF), estimated return for investor (ER), number of pledged investors (PI), project’s creative level (CL), and minimum investment amount per investor (MI). The unit of analysis is crowdfunding projects.
method: Theoretical modeling of optimal decisions for startups and platforms under four crowdfunding mechanisms (equity/debt with All-or-Nothing (AON) or Keep-it-All (KIA) frameworks). Empirical analysis using linear regression and subsample regression analysis to validate theoretical findings.
results: 1. Project creativity's impact on crowdfunding success is heterogeneous: positive for low-return/Art & Craft projects (acts as social currency), but negative for high-return/Technology-Intensive projects (excessive creativity signals over-promising and execution risk, triggering an 'Innovation Paradox'). 2. Optimal matching between project types and crowdfunding mechanisms: Technology-Intensive projects predominantly prefer equity crowdfunding with AON mechanism to mitigate investor skepticism, while Art & Craft projects prefer debt crowdfunding or KIA mechanism to maximize community support. 3. Platform-led creative incentives (cost-sharing policy) can lead to win-win outcomes by improving startup creative levels and profits, with optimal subsidy ratio increasing with the platform’s strategic value. However, a 'Conflict Zone' exists due to divergent optimal incentives for startups and platforms, and feasibility is constrained by commission rates and innovation costs. 4. Backer engagement (number of investors) is positively influenced by creativity in low-return projects but negatively or insignificantly in high-return projects, indicating a 'Dual Effect' where creativity can either build community or act as a risk barrier.

tech_capa_definition: Technology Capability (X_1) emphasizes innovation quality and commercialization readiness.
tech_capa_operationalization: Technology value (X_1-1) is measured using IncoPat's Patent Valuation Algorithm Pass; Technology Readiness Level (TRL) (X_1-2) is categorized into lab, prototype, and commercialization stages; Product richness (X_1-3) is quantified by iterative product updates, emphasizing continuous innovation.
funding_success_definition: The study aims to identify high-potential technology startups (TSs) that are capable of yielding substantial returns for VCs in the near future.
funding_success_operationalization: Funding success is operationalized by ranking Technology Startups (TSs) using a multi-dimensional “technology-market-team” indicator system, evaluated via a combined weighting-TOPSIS approach. TSs are then classified into three categories: Priority Allocation, Prudent Observation, and Risk Mitigation.
signal_vehicles: patent, prototype, founder experience, team composition, research alliances
industry_sector: aerospace, low_altitude_economy, unmanned_aerial_vehicle_flight_control, energy, green_energy, proton_exchange_membrane
data: Patent data for UAV flight control (16,174 unique patent citations) from Derwent Innovations Index (DII) and IncoPat global patent databases. Enterprise data for 148 technology startups (TSs) in China (established after 2020, holding at least one patent, with financing needs, and not Series C or later funded) from Win.d Global Enterprise Database and IT Juzi Database. A cross-domain case study used patent data for Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) technology. The unit of analysis is patents and technology startups.

The research design is a case study of China's unmanned aerial vehicle flight control sector, with a cross-domain validation on PEM technology.
results: The framework identified 8 technology communities in UAV flight control. It uncovered 73 high-potential TSs across seven technology communities, with communities related to flight safety control, specialized configurations, and flight simulation identified as the most promising investment avenues. 11 TSs were categorized into 'Priority Allocation', 21 into 'Prudent Observation', and 41 into 'Risk Mitigation'. A cross-domain validation identified 18 high-potential TSs across three technical communities in Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) technology. The study concludes that the framework provides a scalable, AI-driven decision-support instrument for venture capitalists and policymakers.

tech_capa_definition: Ventures' innovation capability, implicitly linked to invention records and firm quality signals.
tech_capa_operationalization: Number of patents, measured by patent application year, as a proxy for innovation capability.
funding_success_definition: Entrepreneurial firm valuation, specifically pre-money valuation, which is a key factor for VC investors in making investment decisions.
funding_success_operationalization: Pre-money valuation.
signal_vehicles: patent number,founder education,founder financing experience
mediation_moderation_effects: Interaction effect between founder financing experience and patent number (hardly improve valuation); positive interaction effect between syndicate size and VC experience on entrepreneurial firm valuation; complex interaction effects among VC social capital features (e.g., clustering coefficient and structural holes) on firm valuation.
data: VC and entrepreneurial firm data mainly from Thomson Reuters Eikon, patent data from Orbis patent database, and Chinese firm-specific data from Qcc database. The sample consists of 912 unique VC investments into 314 unique Chinese entrepreneurial firms in first-round financing, spanning from 1998 to 2018. The unit of analysis is the firm deal, focusing on China as an emerging market.
method: Integrated machine learning model (Adam-ENN) combining differential evolution algorithm and adaptive learning rate optimization algorithm for neural network training and prediction. Feature contribution analysis uses the partial derivatives method, and relationships/interaction effects are visualized using partial dependence plots (PDPs). Baseline models include Least Squares (LS), Ridge regression, Deep Neural Network (DNN), and Random Forest (RF) algorithms.
results: The integrated machine learning model (Adam-ENN) significantly outperforms baseline models (LS, Ridge, DNN, RF) in predicting early-stage entrepreneurial firm valuation. Feature contribution analysis reveals that syndicate size is the most important feature, followed by VC investors' social capital characteristics. The number of patents is largely ineffective as a signal of firm quality in early-stage valuation and cannot crack the top 10 features, demonstrating that innovation capability seems not so important at nascent stages. Business model performs better than founder financing experience in importance. While individually signals like founder financing experience and patent number show positive relationships with valuation in partial dependence plots, complex nonlinear relationships and interaction effects are observed for features like fund inflows and VC investors' social network characteristics. Specifically, founder financing experience and patent number hardly interact to improve entrepreneurial firm valuation, but syndicate size has a positive interaction effect with VC experience, and complex combined effects exist among syndication network features (structural holes and clustering coefficient).

tech_capa_operationalization: Presence of the firm in one of the seven strategic emerging industries (dummy variable).
funding_success_definition: The likelihood of a project application passing the platform's investigation process and being launched online, which is a prerequisite for acquiring financing.

signal_vehicles: words, images, videos, strategic emerging industry affiliation
mediation_moderation_effects: Repurchasing promises (signal of project risk) fully mediate the negative relationship between start-up credit (signal of start-up quality) and the likelihood of being launched online.
industry_sector: energy_saving_and_environmental_protection, information_technology, biotechnology, high_end_equipment_manufacturing, new_energy, advanced_material, alternative_fuel_vehicles
data: Data collected from Dahuotou equity crowdfunding platform (located in Anhui Province, China) from its establishment in August 2014 to March 2017. The sample consists of 473 projects, where 72 were launched online and 401 were rejected. The unit of analysis is the project, with collected information including equity offerings, financing goal, minimum investment, location, repurchasing promises, industry, and media usage.
method: Logit regression analysis was used to test the hypotheses. Robustness tests involved transforming the geographic distance into a dummy variable, using a 'performance index of firms' as a new indicator of start-up quality, and a dummy variable 'video' for media usage.
results: Equity crowdfunding platforms exhibit local bias, preferring to launch local projects online. A higher quantity of media usage (words, images, and videos) in project applications is positively correlated with the likelihood of being launched online. Projects from firms in strategic emerging industries are more likely to be launched online. Better start-up credit (lower credit ranking) is positively correlated with the likelihood of being launched online. Projects offering repurchasing promises are more likely to be launched online. Furthermore, repurchasing promises fully mediate the positive relationship between start-up credit (quality) and the likelihood of being launched online.

title: Online or not? What factors affect equity crowdfunding platforms to launch projects online in the pre-investment stage?

tech_capa_definition: The paper implicitly conceptualizes 'technology-orientation' as a pursuit of advances in technology and innovations facilitated by advanced digital technologies (ADT). A project is referred to as technology-oriented when an ADT term is explicitly mentioned in its pitch.
tech_capa_operationalization: The 'technology-orientation signal' is operationalized as the number of Advanced Digital Technology (ADT) terms contained per project pitch. A dictionary of 66 unique keywords derived from annual Consumer Electronic Shows (CES, 2015-2019) was established. The variable takes the count of matched keywords between individual project pitches and this ADT dictionary, reflecting the frequency of occurrence of these terms.
funding_success_definition: The paper implicitly defines 'funding success' as achieving better crowdfunding outcomes or successful crowdfunding outcomes.
funding_success_operationalization: The amount of funding raised in dollar amounts is adopted as the dependent variable, reflecting the overall success of a crowdfunding project. Log transformations were applied to account for data skewness.
signal_vehicles: ADT terms,geographic location
mediation_moderation_effects: The paper examines the moderation effects of: crowdfunding platform types on the relationship between technology-orientation and crowdfunding success (H2); geographic location signals on the relationship between technology-orientation and crowdfunding success (H3); project minimum fundraising goals on the relationship between technology-orientation and crowdfunding success (H4); and pitch articulation styles (analytical and authentic) on the relationship between technology-orientation and crowdfunding success (H5, H6).
AI_venture: Artificial intelligence (AI) is explicitly listed as one of the advanced digital technologies (ADT) that promotes business innovation and reshapes the industrial landscape. The study's general findings indicate that technology-oriented projects, which include those featuring AI terms, are more likely to achieve better fundraising outcomes in crowdfunding.
data: Data was collected from six leading US-based crowdfunding platforms (Kickstarter, Indiegogo for reward-based; Microventures, Seedinvest, Wefunder, Fundable for equity-based) between 2015-2020. The sample consists of 2794 crowdfunding projects (13 Microventure, 13 Seedinvest, 342 Wefunder, 67 Fundable, 375 Indiegogo, 1984 Kickstarter). The unit of analysis is crowdfunding projects, with project information and pitches forming the basis of content and sentiment analysis.
method: The study employed an empirical research design using text analysis. Key methods include Computer-Aided Text Analysis (CATA) and Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) for pitch content and sentiment characteristics, univariate tests (paired sample t-tests, independent sample t-tests) for location and quality signals, and multivariate regressions (Models 1, 2, 3) to test hypotheses. Inter-Rater Reliability (IRR) checks were performed for CATA measures.
results: Technology-oriented projects are significantly more likely to achieve higher crowdfunding amounts (H1 supported). Technology-oriented projects are more likely to achieve better crowdfunding performance on reward-based platforms compared to equity-based platforms (H2 supported). Technology-oriented projects, especially on equity-based platforms, tend to gain better fundraising performance with positive geographic location signals (H3 partially supported). Setting a higher-than-average minimum fundraising goal is effective for technology-oriented projects to acquire better crowdfunding performance (H4 partially supported). For technology-oriented projects, a higher authentic articulation tone leads to significantly better crowdfunding outcomes (H6 supported), while a higher analytical tone can backfire, leading to worse outcomes (H5 partially supported with a negative interaction effect).

title: Strategic positioning of projects in crowdfunding platforms: Do advanced technology terms referencing, signaling and articulation matter?

tech_capa_definition: Technological legitimacy relates to the social evaluation and acceptance of a firm based on whether the firm’s technological capabilities can enable a firm to responsively and satisfactorily respond to the audience demand and deliver benefits and utilities through technological advantages and innovation.
tech_capa_operationalization: Home-country technological legitimacy is measured by the Global Innovation Index (GII) developed by the World Intellectual Property Organisation (WIPO). GII is composed of five innovation input conditions (institutions, human capital and research, infrastructure, market sophistication, and business sophistication) and two innovation output conditions (knowledge and technology outputs, and creative outputs), reflecting the cumulative nature of a country’s capabilities and performance in innovation and creativity.
funding_success_definition: Crowdfunding performance or success is conceptualized in two ways: whether the pledged funding goal was met, or the ratio of funds raised to the pledged goal.
funding_success_operationalization: Operationalized as a binary variable: a campaign is coded as “1” if it meets the pledged goal and “0” otherwise. Also measured as a continuous percentage: the ratio between the total funding amount raised by the end of the campaign and the pledged funding goal set by the entrepreneurs.
signal_vehicles: campaign story text
mediation_moderation_effects: Optimism language moderates the negative relationship between home-country technological legitimacy and crowdfunding performance; Insistence language moderates the negative relationship between home-country technological legitimacy and crowdfunding performance; Tenacity language moderates the negative relationship between home-country technological legitimacy and crowdfunding performance; Combined optimism, insistence, and tenacity language moderates the negative relationship between home-country technological legitimacy and crowdfunding performance.
data: Secondary data collected from 758 randomly selected technology-focused crowdfunding campaigns from emerging economies listed on Kickstarter. Projects completed their funding between January 2010 and April 2024. The unit of analysis is the crowdfunding campaign.
method: Logit regression, Generalized Linear Model (GLM) regression, Probit regression, VCE (Robust) and Bootstrapping VCE models for robustness checks, and Quantile analysis. Textual analysis was performed using DICTION 7.0 and LIWC software to extract linguistic variables.
results: Home-country technological legitimacy (measured by GII) has a significant negative association with crowdfunding performance, meaning campaigns from countries with higher GII scores (greater legitimacy) perform better. Positive psychological capital language (optimism, insistence, and tenacity) individually showed a weak positive association with crowdfunding performance. Crucially, these linguistic signals play a moderating role: when optimistic, insistence, or tenacity language is present in campaign stories, it significantly mitigates the negative relationship between lower home-country technological legitimacy and crowdfunding performance, making the relationship positive and statistically significant. The combined use of all three positive psychological capital languages further pronounced this positive association.
